# 30 Days of AI Engineering - Repository Instructions

This repository is a full educational course, not a documentation site. Write every lesson like a polished textbook chapter combined with a university lecture and an interactive workshop.

## Core Mission
- Teach AI Engineering from absolute beginner to advanced practitioner through progressive, project-based learning.
- Make the repository suitable for self-learners, university students, bootcamps, professional engineers, AI engineers, and company training programs.
- Keep the tone friendly, patient, encouraging, professional, and clear.
- Prioritize depth, clarity, and practical understanding over brevity.

## Teaching Philosophy
- Teach concepts progressively and never assume prior knowledge.
- Always explain WHY before HOW.
- Every major topic should answer: what it is, why it exists, what problem it solves, how it works internally, when to use it, when not to use it, advantages, limitations, alternatives, best practices, common mistakes, and real-world use cases.
- Use stories, analogies, visual thinking, practical examples, interactive exercises, and mini projects.
- Students should understand ideas deeply rather than memorize APIs.

## Lesson Structure
Every lesson should generally include:
- Introduction
- Learning objectives
- Prerequisites
- Big picture
- Deep theory
- Visual learning with Mermaid diagrams
- Code walkthrough
- Practical examples
- Best practices
- Common mistakes
- Performance
- Security
- Exercises
- Mini project
- Summary
- Further reading

## Content Quality Standards
- Aim for 3,000 to 6,000+ words per lesson when the topic warrants it.
- Avoid blog-post style, cheat-sheet style, or summary-style writing.
- Include multiple diagrams, comparison tables, code examples, quizzes, and reflection questions where appropriate.
- Explain every important code line and design choice.
- Include beginner, intermediate, advanced, production, and real-world company examples for important concepts.
- Make every lesson feel publishable as a professional technical book chapter.

## Curriculum Progression
- Week 1: absolute beginner foundations.
- Week 2: building AI applications.
- Week 3: knowledge, retrieval, and memory.
- Week 4: agents, evaluation, guardrails, deployment, and capstone work.
- Never introduce a concept before the learner has been prepared for it in earlier lessons.

## Writing Style
- Write in simple English.
- Use rich Markdown with clear headings, tables, bullet lists, notes, tips, and warnings.
- Favor teaching language over reference language.
- Make explanations concrete, intuitive, and actionable.
- Keep the writing style consistent across all days.

## Code Quality
- Every code example should be valid, well structured, and production-minded.
- Use Python and TypeScript where appropriate.
- Include comments only when they add teaching value.
- Include error handling and clear data structures.
- Explain the purpose of each important line.
- Prefer realistic folder structures and workflows.

## Technology Guidance
Use modern AI engineering tools when they make the lesson stronger:
- Python and TypeScript
- OpenAI SDK, Claude SDK, Gemini API
- LangChain and LangGraph
- Model Context Protocol
- FastAPI and Docker
- ChromaDB, Pinecone, Qdrant, Weaviate, FAISS, pgvector
- LiteLLM, OpenRouter, Redis, PostgreSQL
- GitHub Models, Hugging Face, Ollama

Always explain why a technology is chosen, compare alternatives, and mention best practices.

## Repository-Wide Expectations
- Keep each day focused on one learning goal and one mini project.
- Ensure each lesson builds toward the final capstone.
- Preserve the educational tone and progression across the whole repository.
- Keep navigation links consistent between days.
- Prefer improvements that raise instructional quality over cosmetic changes.

## Editing Priorities
- Fix the root cause, not just the surface symptom.
- Keep changes minimal and focused.
- Preserve the existing repository style unless a lesson is clearly too shallow.
- Validate edits with the narrowest useful check after making them.
